facebook.content_angle: The breathing mistake that costs competitive swimmers time — bilateral vs dominant-side breathing and how to choose the right pattern for race day with a drill to master both
facebook.post_text:
The way you breathe in training could be costing you on race day. 🌊

Most swimmers default to one pattern and never question it. Then race day arrives — and oxygen debt, timing gaps, or a blind spot in their stroke kills the split.

Here's what actually matters:
👉 Bilateral breathing builds stroke symmetry and balance — essential in training
👉 Dominant-side breathing delivers more oxygen at race pace — critical in competition
👉 The mistake is treating them as either/or, not as tools for different jobs

The athletes who master both and know when to switch? They race smarter. 🔥

✔️ Train bilaterally at least 3 sessions per week to correct stroke imbalance
✔️ In race-pace sets, practice breathing to your strong side every 2 strokes
✔️ Do 4×50m alternating patterns mid-session — your body will tell you which split is faster

What breathing pattern do you use in races — and did anyone actually teach you why?
facebook.engagement_bait: Drop your breathing pattern below — every 2, every 3, or something else entirely?
